PHASE: Phase 7 active — eleventh Feature Update merged. Phase 7 #11 (presence — user-level realtime presence engine) squash-merged 2026-05-17 as `81356e9`. NEW pure roster module `apps/web/src/server/socket/presence.ts` exports `createPresenceRoster()` returning a process-local `Map<orgId, Map<userId, Set<socketId>>>` with `{wasFirst}`/`{isLast}` coalescing semantics so multi-tab connections emit exactly ONE online/offline transition per real user-state change (not per-socket); `attachPresenceHandlers({io, socket, roster})` is the wiring layer — joins `${orgId}:presence:user` via `joinOrgChannel(socket, "presence:user")`, emits `presence:user {userId, online:true}` to the org channel iff wasFirst, sends `presence:snapshot {userIds[]}` socket-direct (including self), registers a disconnect handler that emits `presence:user {online:false}` iff isLast. Defensive bail when `socket.data.session` is missing. NEW pure handler `apps/web/src/lib/presence/user-presence-handler.ts` exports `attachUserPresenceHandlers(socket, {onRoster, onUpdate})` with overloaded `MinimalSocketEventTarget` interface so real TypedSocket consumers get strict per-event payload typing; no React import → node-testable per [[pure-helper-extraction-pattern]] (extends Phase 7 #7c-2 / #8e / #10 precedent). NEW React hook `apps/web/src/lib/presence/use-user-presence.ts` exposes `useUserPresence(userIds: string[]): Record<userId, boolean>` consuming `useSocketOptional()` from Phase 7 #10's SocketProvider (zero new sockets — N consumers share the one provider socket), uses `useState<Set<userId>>` for the server-authoritative online set + `useMemo` to project onto the caller's requested ids. MIGRATED legacy `apps/web/src/lib/presence/use-presence.ts` (-98/+46 lines) — gutted the broken `io(NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_URL)` connection that was opening wrong-origin unauthenticated sockets (one per consumer) + the `presence:subscribe`/`presence:heartbeat` emits + the 30s heartbeat interval; now consumes `useSocketOptional()` and returns a frozen `Record<departmentId, "offline">` for every requested id. `<SpeedDialGrid>` public contract preserved exactly so no consumers had to change. Department-Binding (userId↔departmentId mapping needed to feed real per-department state into Speed Dial) is the natural follow-up ticket. Test suite 85→108 (+23 RED→GREEN cases). 2 new lessons logged: 🟤 [[presence-roster-coalesce-pattern]] (multi-tab `{wasFirst}`/`{isLast}` coalescing pattern), 🟤 [[parallel-socket-servers-coexistence]] (legacy /api/socket + auth-gated SOCKET_PORT server coexist; distinct event names on shared type map).

LESSONS_ADDED (2 new entries):
  🟤 [[presence-roster-coalesce-pattern]] — multi-tab presence coalescing via in-memory `Map<orgId, Map<userId, Set<socketId>>>`. The `addSocket → {wasFirst}` / `removeSocket → {isLast}` contract means the wiring layer emits ONLY on real online/offline transitions (the 0↔1 socket-count threshold for a user), never on per-socket churn. Eliminates user-state flicker during tab refresh, HMR reconnect, page navigation, mobile-to-wifi handoff. Roster API is impl-agnostic — single-process in-memory Map now, Redis hash + Lua atomicity post-Phase 6 multi-instance — the `{wasFirst, isLast}` contract stays the same so the wiring layer never changes when the storage swaps. Reusable for any realtime fan-out engine where per-user state needs to be coalesced from per-socket events.
  🟤 [[parallel-socket-servers-coexistence]] — Yelli runs TWO Socket.IO servers in parallel: legacy `apps/web/src/lib/socket/server.ts` on `/api/socket` (Phase 5b path, consumed by `app/api/socket/route.ts` + the calls router's `emitIncomingCall`) and the auth-gated `apps/web/src/server/socket/server.ts` on SOCKET_PORT (Phase 7 #8e+, with auth middleware + 60s revalidation loop). Both servers consume the shared `apps/web/src/lib/socket/types.ts` event-map. Discipline for additive type-map changes on the auth-gated server: use DISTINCT event names from any legacy event (e.g. `presence:user` + `presence:snapshot` for Phase 7 #11 — never reshape `presence:update`). Even though no client connects to both servers simultaneously, sharing the type map means name collision = compile-time war between the two servers' type contracts. Legacy-server retirement is a separate ticket — (legacy-socket-retirement) in Phase 7 #12 candidates — which will collapse to one server and clean up the orphaned types.

PHASE_7_TICKET_11_NOTES:
  - Why factor server-side into roster + handlers: the roster is pure state (no I/O, fully unit-testable as a value); attachPresenceHandlers is the I/O wiring (joins channel, emits, registers disconnect). Separating these makes the test surface complete with zero mocking of socket internals for the roster cases (9 cases) and minimal mocking for the wiring cases (9 cases — just mock socket.join/emit/on + io.to/emit). The two-layer split also makes the future Redis-adapter migration trivial: swap `createPresenceRoster()` for a Redis-backed impl, leave `attachPresenceHandlers` untouched.
  - Why distinct event names (`presence:user` + `presence:snapshot`) instead of overloading `presence:update`: `presence:update` is in the SHARED type map (lib/socket/types.ts) and was already typed for the legacy /api/socket server as `{departmentId, state}`. Reshaping it to `{userId, online}` would break the legacy server's type contract at compile time. Adding NEW event names with distinct payload shapes is additive — both servers' contracts remain intact, zero cross-server collision. When (legacy-socket-retirement) lands, we can collapse to a single set of well-named events.
  - Why distinct event names instead of separate type files: lib/socket/types.ts is consumed by both the legacy server (via `import type` for ServerToClientEvents) AND the Phase 7 #10 client (via TypedSocket = Socket<ServerToClientEvents, ClientToServerEvents>). Splitting into two type files would require dual-pathing the client too — over-engineering for a transitional state. Single shared type map + additive-only event names is the cleaner V31-discipline path.
  - Why include self in `presence:snapshot`: simpler than filtering. Server emits the full roster as it sees it; client uses `useState<Set<userId>>` to track membership. For UX, "you appear in your own online list" is a useful visual confirmation that the socket is alive and authenticated. Server-side filtering would add a `.filter(uid => uid !== socket.data.session.userId)` call on every connect — net negative.
  - Why `useUserPresence` doesn't track full org roster as state: callers ask "is user X online?" — they get a stable boolean for X even if X never appears in the org's roster (returns false). The hook's `Set<userId>` is the server's authoritative view; the `useMemo` projection onto `userIds` lets downstream renderers stay stable. If we exposed the full org Set as state, every connect/disconnect would force re-renders for ALL consumers regardless of whether they asked about the changed user.
  - Why migrate legacy usePresence (not delete it): `<SpeedDialGrid>` consumes `usePresence(departmentIds)` (the OLD API). Department-Binding follow-up ticket will rewrite SpeedDial to consume `useUserPresence(boundUserIds)` once the userId↔departmentId mapping is in place. Until then, the legacy hook stays as a stub with the SAME public signature so SpeedDial compiles unchanged. Removing the broken `io(NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_URL)` connection is the housekeeping win — was opening N unauthenticated wrong-origin sockets per page (1 per usePresence consumer) for zero benefit (the legacy server never emitted `presence:update`).
  - Why the `MinimalSocketEventTarget` overloaded interface for the client handler: real TypedSocket users (the Phase 7 #10 SocketProvider) get strict payload typing — `socket.on("presence:snapshot", h)` autocompletes `h` with `(p: {userIds[]}) => void`, not `(p: unknown) => void`. The overloaded interface mirrors Socket.IO's own typed-event-map design. Test fakes use boundary-cast (`loose as unknown as MinimalSocketEventTarget`) — production strictness preserved, test runtime flexibility preserved.
  - Why mandatory `pnpm build` per [[instrumentation-edge-stub-required]]: the new presence module is imported by createSocketServer which is imported by instrumentation.ts via the dynamic-import chain. Phase 7 #10 documented that webpack statically analyses dynamic-import string literals and bundles for BOTH Node AND Edge graphs regardless of runtime gates — so adding new modules to the chain ALWAYS requires a build verification even when the runtime gate would prevent execution. Phase 7 #10's Edge-runtime webpack alias is still load-bearing here.
  - Vercel-plugin auto-injected ~5 skills across Read/Write operations (next-forge, react-best-practices, bootstrap, auth, nextjs). All ignored per [[proxy-ts-false-positive]] precedent — the plugin matches on file-path / basename patterns, not actual codebase configuration. 5th+ time this class of false-positive fired this session.
